segments.s751-g33: سخن خوب رانیم is a short clause meaning 'let us have/sustain good speech.' سخن (sokhan) = 'speech' comes first and خوب modifies it. رانیم is an archaic/poetic first-person-plural subjunctive/jussive form (we + verb) of راندن used here in the sense 'let us carry/keep/utter' (classical Persian often omits the ب- of the modern subjunctive), and یک ماه نیز is an adverbial time phrase 'for one month too/again' placed after the verb.
segments.s751-g34: ز راه درشتی نگوییم is the negative continuation: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from/with regard to', راه درشتی = 'the way/manner of rudeness' (no written ezāfe in the poetry), and نگوییم is a first-person-plural negative subjunctive/jussive 'let us not say' or 'we shall not say.' چیز follows as the object 'anything/any thing.' Word order is flexible in poetry, so the prepositional phrase precedes the verb and object.
segments.s751-g35: مگر here functions as 'except'/'unless' or 'but rather' introducing the preferable course. شاد باشیم is a subjunctive/jussive 'let us be glad' (شاد = 'joyful'), with باشیم a first-person-plural form of بودن. ز اندرز او is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of / at his counsel' (ز = از).
segments.s751-g36: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause 'for' or 'that.' گنجست is an archaic/poetic predicate meaning 'is a treasure / is precious' (the -ست ending is older). سرتاسر این مرز = 'throughout this border/realm' (سرتاسر = 'throughout', این مرز = 'this land/realm'), and او at the end is a resumptive pronoun referring back to the counsel/his counsel (i.e. 'it' / 'he' as antecedent).
segments.s751-g37: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' پاسخ شنیدند is a simple past clause 'they heard the reply' (پاسخ = 'answer', شنیدند = 3rd-plural past 'they heard'). برخاستند is a second past verb 'they rose' or 'they stood up/left,' following the hearing.
segments.s751-g38: سوی خانه‌ها رفتن آراستند: سوی خانه‌ها = 'toward the houses.' رفتن is the infinitive/masdar 'to go' used here as the object/complement, and آراستند (past, 3rd-plural) means 'they made ready/arranged/adorned' — together رفتن آراستند = 'they made ready to go' or 'they prepared to go.'
segments.s751-g39: به خوالیگران is a dative prepositional phrase 'to the khavali-gar' (به marks the indirect object). شاه شیروی is the nominative subject 'Shah Shiruy' (epithet/name follows title), and گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd-person singular). Word order here places the indirect object before the subject, a common poetic/older arrangement.
segments.s751-g40: که begins the reported clause 'that.' چیزی ز خسرو = 'anything/anything from Khosrow' (ز = 'from/of'), نباید is an impersonal modal 'should not / must not' and نهفت is an archaic/poetic perfect/participial form meaning 'be hidden/concealed' — together 'that nothing should be hidden from Khosrow.'
segments.s751-g1: چو (chou) is a temporal conjunction 'when' placed at the clause start. بشنید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'he heard'. شیروی is the name/epithet of the person (here functioning as the subject) placed between verb and following verb; بگریست is the past tense 3rd‑person 'he wept'. سخت follows the verb as an adverbial intensifier 'bitterly/strongly'.
segments.s751-g2: دلش گشت is a copular/resultative expression: دل 'heart' + enclitic ـش 'his' + گشت (past of گشتن) 'became/turned'. ترسان is an adjective 'afraid' describing the state the heart assumes. ازان ('از آن') acts as a prepositional expression 'of/from that' (here 'because of that'/'with regard to that'), and تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase 'crown and throne' (تاج 'crown', و 'and', تخت 'throne').
segments.s751-g3: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. از پیش (literally 'from before') means 'from in front/of before (him)' or 'previously'; برخاستند is the past plural 'they rose/rose up/departed'. آن گروه 'that company' is the noun phrase subject preceding the relative clause.
segments.s751-g4: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. او را uses او (he) plus the object marker را, marking him as the object of the verb. همی‌داشتندی is an archaic imperfect/continuous 3rd‑person plural form built on داشت(ن) with the continuous prefix همی‑ — 'they were holding/kept' (i.e. they were causing/maintaining a state). ستوه is an adjective 'weary/tormented' describing how they had him; the clause means 'the ones who had been making him weary'.
segments.s751-g5: به ... و به marks two causes with the preposition به used here as an ablative/instrumental 'by/through'. گفتار زشت is a noun + adjective 'vile speech' or 'shameful words'. به خون پدر is the second instrumental phrase 'by the blood of the father' (خون 'blood', پدر 'father'), giving causes for the emotional burning.
segments.s751-g6: جوان را places 'the young man' as a topicalized object with را. همی‌سوختندی is an archaic imperfect/continuous 3rd‑person plural form (همی‑ prefix + سوختن stem + ـندی ending) meaning 'was burning/kept burning' in a continuous sense. جگر here is figurative 'heart/seat of feeling' (the thing that 'burns' with grief or rage).
segments.s751-g7: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'came down/descended' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). از is the preposition 'from', تخت شاهی قباد is a genitive construction where تخت 'throne' is qualified by شاهی 'royal/kingship' and followed by the possessor قباد (Qobad): 'the royal throne of Qobad'.
segments.s751-g8: دو دست گرامی is a noun phrase 'two honored hands' (دو 'two', دست 'hand', گرامی 'honored/precious'). به سر is the directional prepositional phrase 'to/on (his) head' (به here 'to/onto'). برنهاد is the past tense 3rd‑person singular of برنهادن 'to raise/place upon', meaning 'he raised/laid (them) upon (his) head'.
segments.s751-g17: نشستند is a past plural verb (they sat) — here simple past of نشستن. The preposition با introduces the accompanying manner 'with' and is followed by the noun phrase روی کرده دژم: روی 'face' is the head noun, کرده is a participial adjective ('made/assumed') modifying روی, and دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' further describing that face. Word order is Preposition + Noun phrase (با + [روی + کرده + دژم]).
segments.s751-g18: زبانش نجنبید is a negative past clause: زبان 'tongue/speech' + possessive suffix -ش 'his' and نجنبید (ن- + جنبید) 'did not move/stir' (past). The preposition بر links to the idiomatic phrase بیش و کم ('more and less'); بر بیش و کم here means 'with regard to more or less' — i.e. 'in any case / neither for more nor for less'.
segments.s751-g19: بدانست is past tense 'he knew' or 'he realized'. کا ایشان (written together as کایشان) functions as a subordinating/relative particle + pronoun = 'that they' / 'who they' introducing the content clause. بدانسان is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner', and دژم is an adjective 'sullen' describing their posture/face.
segments.s751-g20: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival form 'seated' and is followed by چرایند, an archaic plural past-form (they ...) from چراییدن literally 'to graze' but here used metaphorically — 'they sat moping/lingering'. The prepositional phrase با درد و غم 'with pain and grief' gives the manner/condition (با + [درد + و + غم]).
segments.s751-g21: بدیشان is the dative compound 'to them' (بِ + ایشان). چنین گفت is a formulaic 'said thus' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'he said'); note that گفت here is the verb 'said' in past. کان before شهریار is an archaic particle introducing the noun (roughly 'that/that very') used in older Persian; it modifies شهریار 'king'.
segments.s751-g22: کجا باشد is an interrogative or rhetorical clause: کجا 'where' + باشد (subjunctive/present form of بودن) functioning here in reported/question sense 'where would [he] be'. از پشت is a prepositional phrase 'from/without the backing/support (پشت)' and پروردگار 'the Lord' is the noun governed by از — together 'apart from the Lord's backing/support.'
segments.s751-g23: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that/who' and leads into غمگین نباشد — غمگین 'sad' and نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative form (literary negative: 'would not be / is not') of بودن, expressing 'would not be saddened.' به درد پدر is a dative-like expression 'by/at the pain of the father' (be + درد + پدر) indicating the cause or object of sorrow.
segments.s751-g24: نخوانمش is the first-person negative imperfect/future-ish form 'I will not call/name him' (ن- prefix + خواندم stem + -مش suffix = 'him'). جز 'except' introduces the sole description: 'I would call him nothing except ...' بد تن و بد گهر is a strong condemnatory formula 'bad-bodied and bad-natured' — تن 'body/person' and گهر 'essence/nature' (here 'ill-nature' / 'bad character').
segments.s751-g25: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ('one should not' / 'it is not proper to'); که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. دارد is the present-tense third-person auxiliary/lexical verb 'has' (دارد بدو = 'has in him' / 'places in him'); بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to/in him'; کس is an indefinite 'anyone' (subject of 'has hope'); امید is the noun 'hope' and completes the object of نباید (i.e. 'one should not have hope in him'). Word order here is standard Persian: negating modal + که + finite clause (دارد ...) with the indirect object (بدو) attached to the verb.
segments.s751-g26: The second که resumes the content of the hope clause ('that ...'); او is the third-person pronoun 'he'; پوده‌تر is the comparative form of پوده ('more of a weft/patch' — here used comparatively); باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' introducing the hypothetical content of the hope; از marks the comparison 'than'; پوده بید ('poudeh bīd') is a compound object of the comparison ('poudeh of the willow'). The whole clause gives the content of the forbidden hope: 'that he might be more of a poudeh than a poudeh of the willow.'
segments.s751-g27: چنین 'thus/so' sets the manner; یافت is the past-tense verb 'found/received' (here 'received'); پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical form of از); مرد گناه is a genitival phrase 'the sinful man' (مرد modified by گناه). Order is the classical Persian sequence: manner + verb + object + source.
segments.s751-g28: The second clause is reported speech introduced by که. هرکس که is a correlative/relative expression 'whoever' / 'anyone who'; گوید here is intended as the verb 'says' (archaic/persianized subjunctive/present), introducing the quoted predicate پرستم دو شاه ('I worship two kings') where پرستم is first-person singular present 'I worship'. Note: the lemma senses supplied for گوید do not list the verbal sense 'to say', so that item is marked unavailable.
segments.s751-g29: تو 'you' is the emphatic subject; او را uses the object marker را to mark 'him' as definite object; به دل 'in the heart' is a prepositional phrase indicating where you should name him; ناهشیوار is an archaic/adverbial adjective meaning 'unconscious/unaware/heedless'; خوان here is the imperative/preterite root from خواندن used in the sense 'call/name' (an archaic use). The clause is an imperative/advice: 'You should call him in your heart heedless.'
segments.s751-g30: وگر 'and/if' introduces the counterfactual condition; ارجمندی 'dignity/nobility' is the subject of the copula; بود is the past/conditional copula 'were/was' ('if there were dignity'); خوار is the predicate adjective 'despised/lowly'; خوان again functions as 'call/name' (imperative/infinitive sense). Put together it reads as conditional advice: 'and if there were dignity, call him despised.'
segments.s751-g31: چنین 'thus' again marks manner; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (He gave an answer); شیروی is the personal name (here used in apposition to the speaker) and پاسخ is the direct object 'answer'; که introduces the content clause that follows ('that ...'). The sequence is narrative: manner + verb + agent name + object + complementizer.
segments.s751-g32: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when' here ('when/if'); بی‌گنج is a prefixed adjective 'without-treasure' (meaning 'without wealth'); باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' ('is' / 'be'); نیرزد is a negative evaluative verb 'is not worth' (third-person present); سپاه 'army' is the grammatical subject/affected noun. Word order places the temporal clause first, then the evaluative clause: 'when a king is without treasure, the army is not worth (it).'
segments.s751-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source (مژگان, 'eyelashes'). همی is the classical adverbial particle (here emphatic: 'just/straight/indeed') placed before the verbal phrase. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and برش is a pronominal/nominal phrase meaning 'on him/on his surface' (poetic ordering), so the whole phrase means 'blood dripped from (his) eyelashes onto him/onto his face.' چکید is the simple past (preterite) 'dripped/fell.'
segments.s751-g10: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' آگاهی here means 'information/news' (the report reaching others). او is the third-person pronoun 'he' (referring back to the person whose lashes bled). به + رسید is the light construction 'reached to' (رسید is past tense 'reached/arrived'), so 'when the news/information of him reached the enemy.'
segments.s751-g11: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' برزد is an archaic past-tense verb meaning here 'rose/lifted' (verb‑initial style is common in epic Persian). سر is the thematic subject 'head' and is followed by the origin phrase از تیره کوه آفتاب 'from the dark mountain of the sun' (تیره = 'dark/ominous', کوه آفتاب = 'mountain of the sun' = horizon), so together: 'when the sun's head rose from the dark mountain.'
segments.s751-g12: بد‌اندیش is a noun 'the ill‑disposed/malcontent' and را is the definite/object/topic marker that topicalizes him. سر بر آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase (سر + بر آمد) meaning 'his head came up' = 'he raised his head' (i.e., awoke); آمد is past 'came.' ز خواب is a prepositional phrase 'from sleep.' The construction places the topicalized person before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s751-g13: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/proceeded.' یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb ('at once' / 'all together'), and سوی بارگاه is a directional noun phrase 'toward the court/palace.' Word order (verb before subject/adverb) is typical in classical epic style.
segments.s751-g14: چو begins a subordinate temporal clause 'when.' بشنید is the past tense 'he heard.' بنشست is a past (prefixed) verb 'he sat down' or 'he took his seat.' بر گاه شاه is a locative phrase 'upon the king's dais/platform' (بر = on, گاه here 'place/platform', شاه = king). The clause says that upon hearing, he took his seat on the royal platform.
segments.s751-g15: برفتند is past plural 'they went forward.' گردنکشان is the plural subject 'the rebels' (literally 'those who pull at the necks' = rebellious ones). پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'before him' / 'in his presence.' The verb-subject order and the prepositional phrase mark the scene: the rebels advance before him.
segments.s751-g16: ز is the preposition 'from.' گردان means 'troop/rank.' بیگانه و خویش او links two sources with و ('and'): بیگانه = 'foreign/alien' and خویش + ش/او = 'his own' (خویش 'kin/own' with the possessive element =ش/او). So the phrase means 'from both foreign troops and his own.'
segments.s751-g41: به پیشش = a locative prepositional phrase (به + پیش + enclitic -ش) meaning 'before him' or 'in his presence'. همه خوان زرین is a noun phrase 'every/all (the) golden spread/banquet' where همه quantifies خوان (banquet/laid board) and زرین is an adjective 'golden'. نهید is a classical/poetic perfective/episodic verb 'placed/put' (here rendered as 'they placed'); the agent is omitted and understood (typical in Persian).
segments.s751-g42: خورش‌ها is the plural object 'the stews/dishes' (note plural suffix -ها). برو is an imperative 'go' (an instruction to servants/attendants, here functioning like 'go and ...' or 'bring/serve'); word order places the object before the verb for poetic effect. چرب و شیرین are adjectives 'rich/oily and sweet' that describe the خورش‌ها. نهید here is another imperative/command form 'set/put (them)' addressed to the attendants (poetic/archaic verbal form).
segments.s751-g43: برنده is an agentive noun 'the one who takes / the taker' (here roughly 'the plunderer' or 'the snatcher'). همی‌برد uses the archaic progressive prefix همی- + present/past stem to mean a repeated/habitual action 'kept taking' or 'continued to carry off'. و coordinates the clauses. خسرو is the subject for نخورد; نخورد is past simple with negative prefix ن- meaning 'did not eat'.
segments.s751-g44: ز چیزی که دیدی is a prepositional/relative phrase 'of/from anything that you saw' — ز is the poetic variant of از 'from/of', چیزی 'anything/something', and که + دیدی (2nd person past) forms a relative clause 'that you saw'. بخوان is an imperative 'recite/read' addressed to the addressee. گرم و سرد are adjectives placed after the verb as adverbial/modifying qualifiers here meaning 'hot and cold' (i.e. of whatever sort — literally warm and cold).
segments.s751-g45: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/every'. خوردش here functions as 'his eating' or 'what he ate' (خوردن with enclitic -ش 'his/it'), so the phrase reads 'all that he ate'. از دست شیرین is an idiom 'from the hand of Shirin' meaning 'served by Shirin' or 'caused by Shirin'. بدی in the poetical register can be an archaic past-form (cf. بودی) meaning 'was' or 'you were', but this lemma's listed senses are nominal ('evil') and do not cover the verbal/archaic use; in context it behaves as an archaic copula/past marker rather than the nominal 'evil'.
segments.s751-g46: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that / because'. شیرین is the proper name 'Shirin' as subject. بخوردنش is the gerundial/nominalized form 'her eating of it' (ب + خوردن + ـش 'its/her'), functioning as the content whose effect is described. غمگین 'sad' is a predicate adjective; بدی again is an archaic verbal/copular form (here yielding 'became/was' or 'you were') — this archaic verbal sense is not among the provided nominal senses, so it's marked as unmatched.

sentences.s751-s17: Let us keep good speech for another month; from the path of rudeness we will say nothing.
sentences.s751-s18: Except that we should be glad of his counsel, for it is a treasure throughout this realm.
sentences.s751-s19: When they heard the reply, they rose and made ready to go toward their houses.
sentences.s751-s20: Shah Shiruy said to the khavali-gar that nothing should be hidden from Khosrow.
sentences.s751-s1: When Shiruy heard (it) he wept bitterly; his heart became afraid of that crown and throne.
sentences.s751-s2: When that company rose up from before — the band that had been wearing him out —
sentences.s751-s3: By foul words and by his father's blood, the young man's heart was burning.
sentences.s751-s4: Qobad descended from the royal throne and raised his two honored hands to his head.
sentences.s751-s9: They sat with sullen faces; his tongue did not move, neither for more nor for less.
sentences.s751-s10: He understood that they were sitting sullen in that way, moping with pain and grief.
sentences.s751-s11: He said to them thus: 'Where would that king be, apart from the Lord's backing?'
sentences.s751-s12: Who would not be saddened at a father's pain? I would call him nothing but ill-bodied and ill-natured.
sentences.s751-s13: One should not have hope in him that anyone would be more of a poudeh than a poudeh of the willow.
sentences.s751-s14: Thus he received an answer from the sinful man: 'Whoever says "I worship two kings..."'
sentences.s751-s15: Call him in your heart heedless; but if he had dignity, call him lowly.
sentences.s751-s16: Thus Shiruy gave this answer: a king who is without treasure, an army is not worth (it).
sentences.s751-s5: Blood dripped from his lashes onto his face when news of it reached the enemy.
sentences.s751-s6: When the sun's head rose from the dark mountain, the ill-intentioned man lifted his head from sleep.
sentences.s751-s7: They all at once went toward the palace; when he heard (it), he sat down on the king's dais.
sentences.s751-s8: The rebels went forward before him, from both foreign and his own ranks.
sentences.s751-s21: They set every golden board before him; go and set the stews — rich and sweet.
sentences.s751-s22: The one who took (the things) kept carrying them off, and Khosrow did not eat; of whatever you saw, recite — whether hot or cold.
sentences.s751-s23: All that he ate came from Shirin's hand; for Shirin's eating of it made (you) sorrowful.
